author: John Chrysostom
book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

ccel.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

title: Matthew I. 17.

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “So all the generations from Abraham to David are fourteen generations, and from David until the carrying away into Babylon are fourteen generations, and from the carrying away into Babylon unto Christ are fourteen generations.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He hath divided all the generations into three portions, to indicate that not even when their form of government was changed did they become better, but alike under an aristocracy, and under a king, and under an oligarchy, they were in the same evil ways, and whether popular leaders, or priests, or kings controlled them, it was no advantage to them in the way of virtue.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But wherefore hath he in the middle portion passed over three kings, and in the last, having set down twelve generations, affirmed them to be fourteen?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The former question I leave for you to examine;for neither is it needful for me to explain all things to you, lest ye should grow indolent: but the second we will explain.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: To me then he seems in this place to be putting in the place of a generation, both the time of the captivity, and Christ Himself, by every means connecting Him with us.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And full well doth he put us in mind of that captivity, making it manifest that not even when they went down thither, did they become more sober-minded; in order that from everything His coming may be shown to be necessary.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Why then,” one may say, “doth not Mark do this, nor trace Christ’s genealogy, but utter everything briefly?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: It seems to me that Matthew was before the rest in entering on the subject (wherefore he both sets down the genealogy with exactness, and stops at those things which require it): but that Mark came after him, which is why he took a short course, as putting his hand to what had been already spoken and made manifest.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: How is it then that Luke not only traces the genealogy, but doth it through a greater number?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: As was natural, Matthew having led the way, he seeks to teach us somewhat in addition to former statements.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And each too in like manner imitated his master; the one Paul, who flows fuller than any river; the other Peter, who studies brevity.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what may be the reason that Matthew said not at the beginning, in the same way as the prophet, “the vision which I saw,” and “the word which came unto me”?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because he was writing unto men well disposed, and exceedingly attentive to him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For both the miracles that were done cried aloud, and they who received the word were exceeding faithful.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But in the case of the prophets, there were neither so many miracles to proclaim them; and besides, the tribe of the false prophets, no small one, was riotously breaking in upon them: to whom the people of the Jews gave even more heed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This kind of opening therefore was necessary in their case.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if ever miracles were done, they were done for the aliens’ sake, to increase the number of the proselytes; and for manifestation of God’s power, if haply their enemies having taken them captives, fancied they prevailed, because their own gods were mighty: like as in Egypt, out of which no small “mixed multitude”went up; and, after that, in Babylon, what befell touching the furnace and the dreams.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And miracles were wrought also, when they were by themselves in the wilderness; as also in our case: for among us too, when we had just come out of error, many wonderful works were shown forth; but afterwards they stayed, when in all countries true religion had taken root.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what took place at a later periodwere few and at intervals; for example, when the sun stood still in its course, and started back in the opposite direction.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this one may see to have occurred in our case also.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For so even in our generation, in the instance of him who surpassed all in ungodliness, I mean Julian, many strange things happened.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus when the Jews were attempting to raise up again the temple at Jerusalem, fire burst out from the foundations, and utterly hindered them all; and when both his treasurer,and his uncle and namesake, made the sacred vessels the subject of their open insolence, the one was “eaten with worms, and gave up the ghost,”the other “burst asunder in the midst.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Moreover, the fountains failing,when sacrifices were made there, and the entrance of the famine into the cities together with the emperor himself, was a very great sign.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For it is usual with God to do such things; when evils are multiplied, and He sees His own people afflicted, and their adversaries greatly intoxicated with their dominion over them, then to display His own power; which he did also in Persia with respect to the Jews.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore, that he was not acting without an object, or by chance, when he distributed Christ’s forefathers into three portions, is plain from what hath been said.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And mark, too, whence he begins, and where he ends.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: From Abraham to David; from David to the captivity of Babylon; from this unto Christ Himself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For both at the beginning he put the two in close succession, David and Abraham, and also in summing up he mentions both in the same way.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this, because, as I have already said, it was to them that the promises were made.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But why can it be, that as he mentioned the captivity of Babylon, he did not mention also the descent into Egypt?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because they had ceased to be any longer afraid of the Egyptians, but the Babylonians they dreaded still.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And the one thing was ancient, but the other fresh, and had taken place of late.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And to the one they were carried down for no sins, but to the other, transgressions were the cause of their being removed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And also with regard to the very names, if any one were to attempt to translate their etymologies, even thence would he derive great matter of divine speculation,and such as is of great importance with regard to the New Testament: as, for instance, from Abraham’s name, from Jacob’s, from Solomon’s, from Zorobabel’s.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For it was not without purpose that these names were given them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But lest we should seem to be wearisome by running out a great length, let us pass these things by, and proceed to what is urgent.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Having then mentioned all His forefathers, and ending with Joseph, he did not stop at this, but added, “Joseph the husband of Mary;” intimating that it was for her sake he traced his genealogy also.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then, lest when thou hast heard of the “husband of Mary,” thou shouldest suppose that Christ was born after the common law of nature, mark, how he sets it right by that which follows.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Thou hast heard,” saith he, “of an husband, thou hast heard of a mother, thou hast heard a name assigned to the child, therefore hear the manner too of the birth.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “The birth of Jesus Christ was on this wise.”“Of what kind of birth art thou telling me, I pray thee, since thou hast already mentioned His ancestors?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “I still wish to tell thee the manner also of His birth.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou, how he wakens up the hearer?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as though he were about to speak of something unusual,he promises to tell also the manner thereof.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And observe a most admirable order in the things he hath mentioned.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For he did not proceed directly to the birth, but puts us in mind first, how many generations he was from Abraham, how many from David, and from the captivity of Babylon; and thus he sets the careful hearer upon considering the times, to show that this is the Christ who was preached by the prophets.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For when thou hast numbered the generations, and hast learnt by the time that this is He, thou wilt readily receive likewise the miracle which took place in His birth.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus, being about to tell of a certain great thing, His birth of a virgin, he first shadows over the statement, until he hath numbered the generations, by speaking of “an husband of Mary;” or rather he doth even put in short spacethe narration of the birth itself, and then proceeds to number also the years, reminding the hearer, that this is He, of whom the patriarch Jacob had said, He should then at length come, when the Jewish rulers had come to an end; of whom the prophet Daniel had proclaimed beforehand, that He should come after those many weeks.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if any one, counting the years spoken of to Daniel by the angel in a number of weeks, would trace down the time from the building of the city to His birth, by reckoning he will perceive the one to agree with the other.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: How then was He born, I pray thee?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “When as His mother Mary was espoused:” He saith not “virgin,” but merely “mother;” so that his account is easy to be received.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And so having beforehand prepared the hearer to look for some ordinary piece of information, and by this laying hold of him, after all he amazes him by adding the marvellous fact, saying, “Before they came together, she was found with child of the Holy Ghost.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He saith not, “before she was brought to the bridegroom’s house;” for indeed she was therein.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: It being the way of the ancients for the most part to keep their espoused wives in their house:in those parts, at least, where one may see the same practised even now.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus also Lot’s sons-in-law were in his house with him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Mary then herself likewise was in the house with Joseph.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And wherefore did she not conceive before her espousal?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: It was, as I said at first, that what had been done might be concealed awhile, and that the Virgin might escape every evil suspicion.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For when he, who had most right of all to feel jealousy, so far from making her a show, or degrading her, is found even receiving and cherishing her after her conception; it was quite clear that, unless he had fully persuaded himself that what was done was of the operation of the Holy Spirit, he would not have kept her with him, and ministered to her in all other things.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And most properly hath he said, that “she was ‘found’ with child,” the sort of expression that is wont to be used with respect to things strange, and such as happen beyond all expectation, and are unlooked for.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Proceed therefore no further, neither require anything more than what hath been said; neither say thou, “But how was it that the Spirit wrought this of a virgin?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if, when nature is at work, it is impossible to explain the manner of the formation; how, when the Spirit is working miracles, shall we be able to express these?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And lest thou shouldest weary the evangelist, or disturb him by continually asking these things, he hath said who it was that wrought the miracle, and so withdrawn himself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For I know,” saith he, “nothing more, but that what was done was the work of the Holy Ghost.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Shame on them who busy themselves touching the generation on high.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if this birth, which hath witnesses without number, and had been proclaimed so long a time before, and was manifested and handled with hands, can by no man be explained; of what excess of madness do they come short who make themselves busy and curious touching that unutterable generation?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For neither Gabriel nor Matthew was able to say anything more, but only that it was of the Spirit; but how, of the Spirit, or in what manner, neither of them hath explained; for neither was it possible.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nor think that thou hast learnt all, by hearing “of the Spirit;” nay, for we are ignorant of many things, even when we have learnt this; as, for instance, how the Infinite is in a womb, how He that contains all things is carried, as unborn, by a woman; how the Virgin bears, and continues a virgin.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: How, I pray thee, did the Spirit frame that Temple? how did He take not all the flesh from the womb, but a part thereof, and increased it, and fashioned it?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For that He did come forth of the Virgin’s flesh, He hath declared by speaking of “that which was conceived in her;”and Paul, by saying, “made of a woman;” whereby he stops the mouths of themthat say, Christ came among us as through some conduit.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For, if this were so, what need of the womb?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: If this were so, He hath nothing in common with us, but that flesh is of some other kind, and not of the mass which belongs to us.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: How then was He of the root of Jesse?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: How was He a rod? how Son of man? how was Mary His mother? how was He of David’s seed? how did he “take the form of a servant?”how “was the Word made flesh?”and how saith Paul to the Romans, “Of whom as concerning the flesh Christ came, who is God over all?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore that He was of us, and of our substance,and of the Virgin’s womb, is manifest from these things, and from others beside; but how, is not also manifest.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Do not either thou then inquire; but receive what is revealed, and be not curious about what is kept secret.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “And Joseph her husband, being,” saith he “a just man, and not willing to make her a public example, was minded to put her away privily.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Having said that it was of the Holy Ghost, and without cohabitation, he establishes his statement in another way again.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Lest any one should say, “Whence doth this appear?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Who hath heard, who hath seen any such thing ever come to pass?”—or lest you should suspect the disciple as inventing these things to favor his Master;—he introduces Joseph as contributing, by what he underwent, to the proof of the things mentioned; and by his narrative all but says, “If thou doubt me, and if thou suspect my testimony, believe her husband.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For “Joseph,” saith he, “her husband, being a just man.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: By “a just man” in this place he means him that is virtuous in all things.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For both freedom from covetousness is justice, and universal virtue is also justice;and it is mostly in this latter sense that the Scripture uses the name of justice; as when it saith, “a man that was just and true;”and again, “they were both just.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Being then “just,” that is good and considerate, “he was minded to put her away privily.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this intent he tells what took place before Joseph’s being fully informed, that thou mightest not mistrust what was done after he knew.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: However, such a one was not liable to be made a public example only, but that she should also be punished was the command of the law.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Whereas Joseph remitted not only that greater punishment, but the less likewise, namely, the disgrace.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For so far from punishing, he was not minded even to make an example of her.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou a man under self-restraint, and freed from the most tyrannical of passions.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For ye know how great a thing jealousy is: and therefore He said, to whom these things are clearly known, “For full of jealousy is the rage of a husband;”“he will not spare in the day of vengeance:” and “jealousy is cruel as the grave.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And we too know of many that have chosen to give up their lives rather than fall under the suspicion of jealousy.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But in this case it was not so little as suspicion, the burden of the womb entirely convicting her.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But nevertheless he was so free from passion as to be unwilling to grieve the Virgin even in the least matters.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus, whereas to keep her in his house seemed like a transgression of the law, but to expose and bring her to trial would constrain him to deliver her to die; he doth none of these things, but conducts himself now by a higher rule than the law.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For grace being come, there must needs henceforth be many tokens of that exalted citizenship.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as the sun, though as yet he show not his beams, doth from afar by his light illumine more than halfthe world; so likewise Christ, when about to rise from that womb, even before He came forth, shone over all the world.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore, even before her travail, prophets danced for joy, and women foretold what was to come, and John, when he had not yet come forth from the belly, leaped from the very womb.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Hence also this man exhibited great self-command, in that he neither accused nor upbraided, but only set about putting her away.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The matter then being in this state, and all at their wits’ end,the angel comes to solve all their difficulties.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But it is worth inquiring, why the angel did not speak sooner, before the husband had such thoughts: but, “when he thought on it,” not until then, he came; for it is said, “While he thought on these things, the angel” comes.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And yet to her he declares the good tidings even before she conceived.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this again contains another difficulty; for even though the angel had not spoken, wherefore was the Virgin silent, who had been informed by the angel; and why, when she saw her betrothed husband in trouble, did she not put an end to his perplexity?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore then did not the angel speak before Joseph became troubled.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For we must needs explain the former difficulty first.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For what reason then did he not speak?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Lest Joseph should be unbelieving, and the same happen to him as to Zacharias.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For when the thing was visible, belief was thenceforth easy; but when it had not yet a beginning, it was not equally easy to receive his saying.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this reason the angel spake not at the first, and through the same cause the Virgin too held her peace.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For she did not think to obtain credit with her betrothed husband, in declaring to him a thing unheard of, but rather that she should provoke him the more, as though she were cloking a sin that had been committed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since if she herself, who was to receive so great a favor, is affected somewhat after the manner of man, and saith, “How shall this be, seeing I know not a man?”much more would he have doubted; and especially when hearing it from the woman who was under suspicion.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore the Virgin saith nothing to him, but the angel, the time demanding it, presents himself to him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why then, it may be asked, did he not so in the Virgin’s case also, and declare the good tidings to her after the conception?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Lest she should be in agitation and great trouble.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For it were likely that she, not knowing the certainty, might have even devised something amiss touching herself, and have gone on to strangle or to stab herself, not enduring the disgrace.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For wondrous indeed was that Virgin, and Luke points out her excellency, saying, that when she heard the salutation, she did not straightway pour herself out,neither did she accept the saying, but “was troubled,” seeking “what manner of salutation this might be.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now she who was of such perfect delicacy would even have been distracted with dismay at the thought of her shame, not expecting, by whatever she might say, to convince any one who should hear of it, but that what had happened was adultery.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore to prevent these things, the angel came before the conception.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Besides that, it was meet that womb should be free from trouble which the Maker of all things entered; and the soul rid of all perturbation, which was thought worthy to become the minister of such mysteries.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For these reasons He speaks to the Virgin before the conception, but to Joseph at the time of travail.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this many of the simpler sort, not understanding, have said there is a discordance; because Luke saith it was Mary to whom he declared the good tidings, but Matthew, that it was Joseph; not knowing that both took place.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this sort of thing it is necessary to bear in mind throughout the whole history; for in this way we shall solve many seeming discordances.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The angel then comes, when Joseph is troubled.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For in addition to the causes mentioned, with a view also to the manifestation of his self-command, he defers his coming.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But when the thing was on the point of taking place, then at last he presents himself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “While he thought on these things, an angel appeareth to Joseph in a dream.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou the mildness of the husband?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So far from punishing, he did not even declare it to any one, no not even to her whom he suspected, but was thinking it over with himself, as aiming to conceal the cause even from the Virgin herself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For neither is it said that he was minded to “cast her out,” but to “put her away,” so very mild and gentle was the man.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But while he is thinking on these things, the angel appeareth in a dream.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And why not openly, as to the shepherds, and to Zacharias, and to the Virgin?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The man was exceedingly full of faith, and needed not this vision.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Whereas the Virgin, as having declared to her very exceeding good tidings, greater than to Zacharias, and this before the event, needed also a marvellous vision; and the shepherds, as being by disposition rather dull and clownish.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But this man, after the conception,and wide the interval between the two men; wherefore neither was there need of rebuke.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But by saying, “fear not,” he signifies him to have been afraid, lest he should give offense to God, as retaining an adulteress; since, if it had not been for this, he would not have even thought of casting her out.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: In all ways then he points out that the angel came from God, bringing forward and setting before him all, both what he thought to do, and what he felt in his mind.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now having mentioned her name, he stayed not at this, but added also, “thy wife;” whereas he would not have called her so, if she had been corrupted.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And here he calls her that is espoused “a wife;” as indeed the Scripture is wont to call betrothed husbands sons-in-law even before marriage.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But what means, “to take unto thee?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: To retain her in his house, for in intention she had been now put away by him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Her, being put away, do thou retain,” saith he, “as committed unto thee by God, not by her parents.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And He commits her not for marriage; but to dwell with thee; and by my voice doth He commit her.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Much as Christ Himself afterwards committed her to His disciple, so even now unto Joseph.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then having obscurely signified the matter in hand, he mentioned not the evil suspicion; but, in a manner more reverent and seemly, by telling the cause of travail he removed this also; implying that the very thing which had made him afraid, and for which he would have cast her out,—this very thing, I say, was a just cause why he should take her and retain her in his house.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus more than entirelydoing away with his distress.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For she is not only free,” saith he, “from unlawful intercourse, but even above all nature is her conception.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Not only therefore put away thy fear, but even rejoice more exceedingly, ‘for that which is conceived in her is of the Holy Ghost.’”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: A strange thing it was which he spake of, surpassing man’s reason, and above all the laws of nature.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: How then is he to believe, to whom such tidings are altogether new?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “By the things that are past,” saith he, “by the revelations.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For with this intent he laid open all things that were in his mind, what he felt, what he feared, what he was resolved to do;—that by these he might assure himself of this point.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Or rather, not by things past only, but like wise by things to come, he wins him over.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “And she shall bring forth,” saith he, “a Son, and thou shalt call His name Jesus.”“For do not thou, because He is of the Holy Ghost, imagine that thou art an alien to the ministry of this dispensation.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since although in the birth thou hast no part, but the Virgin abode untouched, nevertheless, what pertains to a father, not injuring the honor of virginity, that do I give thee, to set a Name on that which is born: for “thou shalt call Him.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For though the offspring be not thine, yet shalt thou exhibit a father’s care towards Him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore I do straightway, even from the giving of the name, connect thee with Him that is born.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then lest on the other hand any one should from this suspect him to be the father, hear what follows, with what exact care he states it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “She shall bring forth,” he saith, “a Son:” he doth not say, “bring forth to thee,” but merely “she shall bring forth,” putting it indefinitely:since not to him did she bring forth, but to the whole world.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this cause too the angel came bringing His name from Heaven, hereby again intimating that this is a wondrous birth: it being God Himself who sends the name from above by the angel to Joseph.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For neither was this without an object, but a treasure of ten thousand blessings.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore the angel also interprets it, and suggests good hopes, in this way again leading him to belief.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For to these things we are wont to be more inclined, and therefore are also fonder of believing them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So having established his faith by all, by the past things, by the future, by the present, by the honor given to himself, he brings in the prophet also in good time, to give his suffrage in support of all these.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But before introducing him, he proclaims beforehand the good things which were to befall the world through Him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what are these?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Sins removed and done away.“For He shall save His people from their sins.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Here again the thing is signified to be beyond all expectation.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For not from visible wars, neither from barbarians, but what was far greater than these, from sins, he declares the glad tidings of deliverance; a work which had never been possible to any one before.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But wherefore, one may ask, did he say, “His people,” and not add the Gentiles also?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: That he might not startle the hearer yet a while.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For to him that listens with understanding he darkly signified the Gentiles too.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For “His people” are not the Jews only, but also all that draw nigh and receive the knowledge that is from Him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And mark how he hath by the way discovered to us also His dignity, by calling the Jewish nation “His people.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this is the word of one implying nought else, but that He who is born is God’s child, and that the King of those on high is the subject of his discourse.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: As neither doth forgiving sins belong to any other power, but only to that single essence.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Forasmuch then as we have partaken of so great a gift, let us do everything not to dishonor such a benefit.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if even before this honor, what was done was worthy of punishment, much more now, after this unspeakable benefit.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this I say not now for no cause,but because I see many after their baptism living more carelessly than the uninitiated, and having nothing peculiar to distinguish them in their way of life.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: It is, you see, for this cause, that neither in the market nor in the Church is it possible to know quickly who is a believer and who an unbeliever; unless one be present at the time of the mysteries, and see the one sort put out, the others remaining within.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Whereas they ought to be distinguished not by their place, but by their way of life.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as men’s outwarddignities are naturally to be discovered by the outward signs with which they are invested, so ours ought to be discernible by the soul.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: That is, the believer ought to be manifest not by the gift only, but also by the new life.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The believer ought to be the light and salt of the world.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But when thou dost not give light even to thyself, neither bind up thine own gangrene, what remains, whereby we are to know thee?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because thou hast entered the holy waters?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, this to thee becomes a storeof punishment.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For greatness of honor is, to them who do not choose to live worthy of the honor, an increase of vengeance.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, the believer ought to shine forth not only by what he hath received from God, but also by what he himself hath contributed; and should be discernible by everything, by his gait, by his look, by his garb, by his voice.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this I have said, not that display, but that the profit of beholders, may be the rule by which we frame ourselves.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But now, what things soever I might seek to recognize thee by, I find thee in all points distinguished by the contraries of the same.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For whether by thy place I would fain discern thee, I see thee spending thy day in horse races, and theatres, and scenes of lawlessness, in the wicked assemblies in the market places, and in companies of depraved men; or by the fashion of thy countenance, I see thee continually laughing to excess, and dissolute as a grinningand abandoned harlot; or by thy clothes, I see thee in no better trim than the people on the stage; or by thy followers, thou art leading about parasites and flatterers; or by thy words, I hear thee say nothing wholesome, nothing necessary, nothing of moment to our life; or by thy table, yet heavier from thence will the charge against thee appear.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: By what then, tell me, am I to recognize the believerin thee, while all the things I have mentioned give the contrary sentence?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And why do I say, the believer? since I can not clearly make out whether thou art a man.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For when thou art like an ass, kicking, and like a bull, wantoning, and like a horse neighing after women; when thou dost play the glutton like the bear, and pamper thy flesh as the mule, and bear malice like the camel;also?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Further, if I were bidding thee make another man gentle, not even so ought I to seem as one enjoining impossible things; however, thou mightest then object that thou hast not the control of another’s disposition, and that it doth not altogether rest with thee.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But now it is thine own wild beast, and a thing which absolutely depends on thee.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What plea then hast thou? or what fair excuse wilt thou be able to put forth, turning as thou art a lion into a man, and regardless that thou thyself art of a man becoming a lion; upon the beast bestowing what is above nature, but for thyself not even preserving what is natural?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, while the wild beasts are by thine earnest endeavors advanced into our noble estate, thou art by thyself cast down from the throne of the kingdom, and thrust out into their madness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus, imagine, if thou wilt, thy wrath to be a kind of wild beast, and as much zeal as others have displayed about lions, so much do thou in regard of thyself, and cause that way of taking thingsto become gentle and meek.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because this too hath grievous teeth and talons, and if thou tame it not, it will lay waste all things.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For not even lion nor serpent hath such power to rend the vitals as wrath, with its iron talons continually doing so.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since it mars, we see, not the body only, but the very health likewise of the soul is corrupted by it, devouring, rending, tearing to pieces all its strength, and making it useless for everything.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if a man nourishing worms in his entrails, shall not be able so much as to breathe, his inward parts all wasting away; how shall we, having so large a serpent eating up all within us (it is wrath I mean), how, I say, shall we be able to produce anything noble?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: How then are we to be freed from this pest?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: If we can drink a potion that is able to kill the worms within us and the serpents.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “And of what nature,” it will be asked, “may this potion be, that hath such power?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The precious Blood of Christ, if it be received with full assurance,(for this will have power to extinguish every disease); and together with this the divine Scriptures carefully heard, and almsgiving added to our hearing; for by means of all these things we shall be enabled to mortify the affections that mar our soul.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And then only shall we live; for now surely we are in no better state than the dead: forasmuch as it cannot be, that while those passions live, we should live too, but we must necessarily perish.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And unless we first kill them here, they will be sure to kill us in the other life; or rather before that death they will exact of us, even here, the utmost penalty.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yes, for every such passion is both cruel and tyrannical and insatiable, and never ceases to devour us every day.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For “their teeth are the teeth of a lion,”or rather even far more fierce.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the lion, as soon as ever he is satisfied, is wont to leave the carcass that hath fallen in his way; but these passions neither are satisfied, nor do they leave the man whom they have seized, until they have set him nigh the devil.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For so great is their power, that the very service which Paul showed forth to Christ,despising both hell and the kingdom for His sake, even this same do they require of them whom they have seized.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For whether it be with the love of women, or of riches, or of glory, that any one is entangled, he laughs at hell thenceforth, and despises the kingdom, that he may work the will of these.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us not then doubt Paul when he saith that he so loved Christ.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For when some are found so doing service to their passions, how should that other afterwards seem incredible?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, and this is the reason why our longing for Christ is feebler, because all our strength is consumed on this love, and we rob, and defraud, and are slaves to vainglory; than which what can be more worthless?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For though thou shouldest become infinitely conspicuous, thou wilt be nothing better than the base: rather for this selfsame cause thou wilt even be baser.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For when they who are willing to give thee glory, and make thee illustrious, do for this very cause ridicule thee, that thou desirest the glory which comes of them, how can such instances fail to turn the contrary way in regard of thee.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed this thing is among those which attract censure.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So that even as in the case of one desiring to commit adultery or fornication, should any one praise or flatter him, by this very act he becomes an accuser rather than a commender of the person indulging such desires: so with regard to him who is desirous of glory; when we all praise, it is accusation rather than praise which we bestow on those who wish to be made glorious.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why then bring upon thyself that, from which the very opposite is wont to befall thee.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, if thou wilt be glorified, despise glory; so shalt thou be more illustrious than any.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why feel as Nebuchadnezzar felt?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For he too set up an image, thinking from wood and from a senseless figure to procure to himself an increase of fame, and the living would fain appear more glorious by the help of that which hath no life.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou the excess of his madness; how, thinking to do honor, he rather offered insult, to himself?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For when it appears that he is relying rather on the lifeless thing, than on himself and the soul that lives in him, and when for this cause he advances the stock unto such high precedence, how can he be other than ridiculous, endeavoring as he doth to adorn himself, not by his way of living, but by planks of wood?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Just as if a man should think proper to give himself airs, because of the pavement of his house, and his beautiful staircase rather than because he is a man.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Him do many too amongst us imitate now.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as he for his image, so some men claim to be admired for their clothes, others for their house; or for their mules and chariots, and for the columns in their house.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For inasmuch as they have lost their being as men, they go about gathering to themselves from other quarters such glory as is full of exceeding ridicule.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But as to the noble and great servants of God, not by these means, but by such as best became them, even by such did they shine forth.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For captives as they were, and slaves, and youths, and strangers, and stripped of all resources of their own, they proved at that time far more awful than he who was invested with all these things.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And while Nebuchadnezzar found neither so great an image, nor satraps, nor captains of the host, nor endless legions, nor abundance of gold, nor other pomp, enough to meet his desire, and to show him great; to these, on the other hand, stripped of all this, their high self-restraint alone was sufficient, and showed him that wore the diadem and the purple, as much inferior in glory to those who had no such thing, as the sun is more glorious than a pearl.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For they were led forth in the midst of the whole world, being at once youths, and captives, and slaves, and straightway on their appearance the king darted fire from his eyes, and captains, and deputies, and governors, and the whole amphitheatre of the devil, stood around; and a voice of pipes from all sides, and of trumpets, and of all music, borne up to Heaven, was sounding in their ears, and the furnace burned up to a boundless height, and the flame reached the very clouds, and all was full of terror and dismay.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But none of these things dismayed them, but they laughed it all to scorn, as they would children mocking them, and exhibited their courage and meekness, and uttering a voice clearer than those trumpets, they said, “Be it known unto thee, O king.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For they did not wish to affront the king, no not so much as by a word, but to declare their religiononly.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For which cause, neither did they extend their speech to any great length, but set forth all briefly; “For there is,” say they, “a God in Heaven, who is able to deliver us,”“why showest thou me the multitude? why the furnace? why the sharpened swords? why the terrible guards? our Lord is higher and more mighty than all these.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then when they considered that it was possible that God might be willing even to permit them to be burnt; lest, if this should come to pass, they might seem to be speaking falsehoods; they add this also and say, “If this happen not, be it known unto thee, O king, that we serve not thy gods.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For had they said, “Sins are the cause of His not delivering us, should He fail to deliver,” they would not have been believed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore in this place they are silent on that subject, though they speak of it in the furnace, again and again alleging their sins.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But before the king they say no such thing; only, that though they were to be burnt, they would not give up their religion.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For it was not for rewards and recompenses that they did what they did, but out of love alone; and yet they were in captivity too, and in slavery, and had enjoyed no good thing.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, they had lost their country, and their freedom, and all their possessions.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For tell me not of their honors in the king’s courts, for holy and righteous as they were, they would have chosen ten thousand times rather to have been beggars at home, and to have been partakers of the blessings in the temple.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For I had rather,” it is said, “be an outcastin the house of my God, than to dwell in the tents of sinners.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And “one day in thy courts is better than thousands.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: They would have chosen then ten thousand times rather to be outcasts at home, than kings in Babylon.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this is manifest, from what they declare even in the furnace, grieving at their continuance in that country.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For although themselves enjoyed great honors, yet seeing the calamities of the rest they were exceedingly vexed; and this kind of thing is most especially characteristic of saints, that no glory, nor honor, nor anything else should be more precious to them than their neighbor’s welfare.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: See, for example, how even when they were in a furnace, they made their supplication for all the people.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But we not even when at large bear our brethren in mind.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And again, when they were inquiring about the dreams,they were looking “not to their own but the common good,”for that they despised death they showed by many things afterwards.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But everywhere they put themselves forward, as wishing to prevailwith God by importunity.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Next, as not accounting themselves either to be sufficient, they flee to the Fathers; but of themselves they said that they offer nothing more than “a contrite spirit.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: These men then let us also imitate.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because now too there is set up a golden image, even the tyranny of Mammon.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But let us not give heed to the timbrels, nor to the flutes, nor to the harps, nor to the rest of the pomp of riches; yea, though we must needs fall into a furnace of poverty, let us choose it, rather than worship that idol, and there will be “in the midst a moist whistling wind.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us not then shudder at hearing of “a furnace of poverty.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For so too at that time they that fell into the furnace were shewn the more glorious, but they that worshipped were destroyed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Only then all took place at once, but in this case some part will be accomplished here, some there, some both here and in the day that is to come.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For they that have chosen poverty, in order that they might not worship mammon, will be more glorious both here and then, but they that have been rich unjustly here, shall then pay the utmost penalty.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: From this furnace Lazarus too went forth, not less glorious than those children; but the rich man who was in the place of them that worshipped the image, was condemned to hell.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed what we have now mentioned was a type of this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore as in this instance they who fell into the furnace suffered no hurt, but they who sat without were laid hold of with great fierceness, so likewise shall it be then.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The saints walking through the river of fire shall suffer no pain, nay they will even appear joyous; but they that have worshipped the image, shall see the fire rest upon them fiercer than any wild beast, and draw them in.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So that if any one disbelieves hell, when he sees this furnace, let him from the things present believe things to come, and fear not the furnace of poverty, but the furnace of sin.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this is flame and torment, but that, dewand refreshment; and by this stands the devil, by that, angels wafting aside the flame.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: These things let them hear that are rich, that are kindling the furnace of poverty.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For though they shall not hurt those others, “the dew”coming to their aid; yet themselves they will render an easy prey to the flame, which they have kindled with their own hands.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then, an angel went down with those children; now, let us go down withthem that are in the furnace of poverty, and by alms-deeds let us make a “dewy air,”and waft the flame quite aside, that we may be partakers of their crowns also; that the flames of hell may likewise be scattered by the voice of Christ saying, “Ye saw me an hungered, and fed me.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For that voice shall then be with us instead of a “moist wind whistling”through the midst of the flame.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us then go down with alms-giving, unto the furnace of poverty; let us behold them that in self-restraint walk therein, and trample on the burning coals; let us behold the marvel, strange and beyond thought, a man singing praise in a furnace, a man giving thanks in fire, chained unto extreme poverty, yet offering much praise to Christ.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since they, who bear poverty with thankfulness, really become equal to those children.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For no flame is so terrible as poverty, nor so apt to set us on fire.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But those children were not set on fire; rather, on their giving thanks to the Lord, their bonds too were at once loosed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So likewise now, if when thou hast fallen into poverty, thou art thankful, both the bonds are loosened, and the flame extinguished; or though it be not extinguished (what is much more marvellous), it becomes a fountain instead of a flame: which then likewise came to pass, and in the midst of a furnace they enjoyed a pure dew.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the fire indeed it quenched not, but the burning of those cast in it altogether hindered.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This one may see in their case also who live by the rules of wisdom,for they, even in poverty, feel more secure than the rich.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us not therefore sit down without the furnace, feeling no pity towards the poor; lest the same befall us as then befell those executioners.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if thou shouldest go down to them, and take thy stand with the children, the fire will no longer work thee any harm; but if thou shouldest sit above and neglect them in the flame of their poverty, the flame will burn thee up.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Go down therefore into the fire, that thou mayest not be burnt up by the fire; sit not down without the fire, lest the flame catch hold of thee.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if it should find thee amongst the poor, it will depart from thee; but if alienated from them, it will run upon thee quickly, and catch thee.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Do not therefore stand off from them that are cast in, but when the devil gives command to cast them that have not worshipped gold into the furnace of poverty, be not thou of them that cast others in, but of them that are cast in; that thou mayest be of the number of the saved, and not of the burned.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed it is a most effectual dew, to be held in no subjection by desire of wealth, to be associate with poor persons.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: These are wealthier than all, who have trampled under foot the desire of riches.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Forasmuch as those children too, by despising the king at that time, became more glorious than the king.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And thou therefore, if thou despise the things of the world, shalt become more honorable than all the world; like those holy men, “of whom the world was not worthy.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: In order then to become worthy of the things in Heaven, I bid thee laugh to scorn things present.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For in this way thou shalt both be more glorious here, and enjoy the good things to come, by the grace and love towards man of our Lord Jesus Christ; to whom be glory and might for ever and ever.
